title: Ful medames
subtitle: Egyptian stewed fava beans with lemon and cumin
excerpt: Dried fava beans soaked overnight and slow-cooked until completely soft, then mashed roughly and finished with lemon juice, cumin, garlic, and olive oil. One of the oldest continuously eaten dishes in the world, and still the most common breakfast in Egypt and across much of the Arab world. Served with hard-boiled eggs, pitta, and fresh vegetables.
sourceNotes: Ful medames is Egypt's national dish and the most widely eaten breakfast in the Arab world. Claudia Roden's A Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) documents its appearance across Egypt, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, and Sudan. The dish has been traced to ancient Egypt, with records of fava bean cultivation along the Nile dating to at least 2500 BCE. The modern form, cooked in a large pot overnight and sold by street vendors at dawn, has been a Cairo institution for centuries.

content.text: Ful medames is slow-cooked fava beans, roughly mashed and finished with lemon, raw garlic, and olive oil. Egypt's most common breakfast, eaten with hard-boiled eggs, warm pitta, sliced tomato, and cucumber. The traditional method cooks the beans overnight in a sealed pot; at home, a steady simmer for 1 to 1.5 hours does the same job.

The beans must be completely soft before you season them: breaking down and yielding when pressed. Undercooked ful is grainy and dense. The finishing is everything: the beans are bland on their own; lemon, raw garlic, and good olive oil transform them. Season assertively.

items.name: Dried fava beans
items.prepNote: soaked overnight in cold water, drained

content.text: Drain the soaked {{fava-beans-dried}} and put them in a large saucepan. Cover well with cold water. Bring to the boil and skim off any foam.

Reduce to a steady simmer and cook for 1 to 1.5 hours until the beans are very soft and some are starting to collapse. Top up with boiling water if the level drops below the beans. Do not add salt until the end: salting too early toughens the skins.
Drain the beans, keeping some cooking liquid. Return them to the pan. Mash roughly with a fork or the back of a spoon, leaving about half the beans whole for texture.

Stir in the {{garlic}}, {{lemon}} juice, {{cumin-ground}}, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, and salt. Add a splash of cooking liquid if it is too thick. Taste and adjust: the ful should be deeply savoury, sharp with lemon, and fragrant with cumin.
Serve in bowls topped with {{parsley-flat}}, a drizzle of extra olive oil, and {{chilli-flakes}} if using. Place the hard-boiled {{eggs}} alongside with pitta, sliced tomato, and cucumber.

items.fix: Dried legumes can take longer if old. Soak for a full 12 hours. Continue cooking, checking every 15 minutes. Very old beans can take 2 hours or more
items.cause: Old dried beans or not soaked long enough
items.symptom: Beans are still firm after 1.5 hours

items.fix: Raw garlic is the key; cooked garlic gives a very different result. Add the garlic at the end with the lemon. Taste and keep adding lemon until the flavour is bright
items.cause: Not enough lemon or garlic, or garlic was cooked rather than raw

items.fix: Drain excess cooking liquid before seasoning. The texture should be porridge-like: spoonable but not soupy. Mash more beans to thicken
items.cause: Too much water left in the pot at the end
items.symptom: Beans are watery rather than thick

items.fix: The skins of small fava beans are usually fine. Larger beans may need their skins slipped off after cooking: pinch each bean and the skin should slide free when fully cooked
items.cause: Old beans or cooking time too short

content.text: Ful medames is the most important breakfast food in Egypt and one of the defining dishes of Arab food culture. In Cairo it is sold by street vendors from large copper pots that have been cooking through the night, ladled out at dawn to workers, students, and anyone passing. The name 'medames' means 'buried' in Coptic, referring to the ancient practice of slow-cooking the beans in clay pots in the embers of cooling bread ovens. Ful crosses religious and class lines in Egypt: eaten by everyone, rich or poor, as a morning meal. The versions vary by region and family, some almost smooth, some chunky, some finished with tomato or tahini or hot sauce. The constant is the fava bean, lemon, and cumin.
